@lazka FOUND IT!

Okay, I have found that commenting out these lines in ``backends/backend_gtk3cairo.py`` seems to fix it (and the same lines in ``backend_gtk3agg.py``):
```python
if HAS_CAIRO_CFFI:
  ctx = cairo.Context._from_pointer(
    cairo.ffi.cast('cairo_t **', id(ctx) + object.__basicsize__)[0],
    incref=True)
```

That call passes, with the segfault occurs after this line has executed, so not exactly sure why commenting this out allows it to work... maybe it circumvented some logic in pgi... I noticed that before calls from pgi would return a ``<cdata 'struct _cairo *' 0x...>`` instead of a ``<cdata 'void *' 0x...> which pgi normally returns.

Next step, as a cairo[cffi] noob, I shall find out why we put those lines in, and if you have any ideas why those lines break the pgi implementation... this should help us decide on whether we need to fix ``pgi`` or fix ``matplotlib`` or both.

Original comment by: Fabian Barkhau

Hi. Sorry for the trouble.

Since version 3 of the library (see [the changelog](/jaraco/irc/src/5591b414bff0855b270750585a183c8fdc39fe90/CHANGES.rst) for details), the IRC library tries to provide a higher level interface, abstracting encoding and decoding of strings. By default, it assumes the server is using UTF-8 encoding.

If you want to restore the previous behavior, where the library just passes through the raw bytestream, there's a workaround indicated in the changelog. Let me know if that doesn't straighten things out for you or if you have additional questions.

As for the logging, I suspect that the logging level isn't set low enough to allow the messages to pass. Can you try:

```
irc.client.log.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
```

in addition to what you've already specified?

This behaviour is actually expected. If you kill ffmpeg process it will exit with code 255 (easily reproducible in the terminal).
I don't think introducing a method to kill the process is a good idea though. Perhaps it's best to leave this to be dealt with by the user. What you could do in this case is catch the exception and check its `exit_code` attribute and ignore the exception based on its value.

Maybe I could split it into two functions.  A `tcp_server_socket()` function that would make a TCP socket. And a `run_server()` function that would run the server.   So, you'd do this:

     sock = await tcp_server_socket(address, ...)
     # Look at sock metadata. Set additional options
     ...
     await run_server(sock, handler)

I'd keep `tcp_server()` the same as it is now (but make it combine these two steps).

As far as I can see the usage of the AWAIT function would be fine wrt local state as it immediately schedules the coroutine to be ran on the kernel thread, whereas an `await coro` doesn't schedule it until a yield point where there might be multiple layers of local state reading/writing.

My hypothetical PR was fundamentally exactly the same as the current implementation, with running the coroutine manually and sending yields to/from the kernel thread.

I think typically the warnings against using `shell=True` are about running untrusted code. Since this is not a webservice, I think this is not a problem.

I've also found another case where the current invocation of `Popen` results in unexpected behaviour: Passing the linker an rpath argument: `-Wl,-rpath='$ORIGIN'`. Without `shell=True`, the single quotes are not evaluated, and the final RPATH in the executable becomes `'$ORIGIN'`, which doesn't work.

Ok, looked at it again and I am not sure I can use it in a way that its already been used.

Reason being that `escape` argument of `dumps_list` controls the whole list escaping.

In this case say you have:
```
Math(data=[Matrix(M), 'string \\'])
```
You do not want to affect Matrix behavior, only string as user/developer should make sure that matrix is correctly escaped before using it.

I have made use of `escape` property, but not in the way it was probably intended. But still a bit nicer than it was, maybe...

> Can Boss send and receive Third request without waiting for timeout of Second request ends?

Yes, but we need an event to break out of the waiting main loop e.g. `run_dispatcher()` for a moment. The most obvious solution is to have Boss registering itself with pysnmp *timer*:

```
def timer_callback(now):
    # potentially, call schedule_get_request() for third request

self.__snmp_engine.transportDispatcher.registerTimerCbFun(timer_callback, tickInterval=0.5):
```

This way, while being in `run_dispatcher()` waiting for response, `timer_callback` will be called every 0.5 sec. Here you could check with Boss if they want to send third request and schedule sending it if so.

Thanks for the report. Yes, it's true that Popen doesn't work as expected with PATHEXT. A [search of PATHEXT](http://bugs.python.org/issue?%40columns=id%2Cactivity%2Ctitle%2Ccreator%2Cassignee%2Cstatus%2Ctype&%40sort=-activity&%40filter=status&%40action=searchid&ignore=file%3Acontent&%40search_text=PATHEXT&submit=search&status=-1%2C1%2C2%2C3) on bugs.python.org yields quite a few related issues.

As I understand it (though I have no reference), Microsoft has commented on this issue and has indicated that a fix to generally support PATHEXT in the CreateProcess API (which Popen uses) is unlikely to happen, and that wrapper executables should be used for maximum compatibility.

I believe there is room for the Python code to work around this limitation, and have Popen resolve the PATHEXT to the hosting executable and run the program that way, but that would be outside the scope of hgtools.

Another option might be to invoke Popen with shell=True, which will use a shell process such as cmd.exe to resolve the PATHEXT script. I'm reluctant to simply make this change as I'm unaware what other implications that might have.

As this project is largely defunct in deference to [setuptools_scm](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/setuptools_scm), I suggest the issue be taken up there. If the issue is fixed there, then I'd be happy to accept the same behavior for this library.

In the meantime, I'm marking this as wontfix, as I won't have time to invest in troubleshooting or testing, but I welcome pull requests.

@lmr2391 I would recommend taking advantage of the type annotations available in Python to help with cd.shared variable completions. If you are using Python 3.6 you can do something like this:

```
import cauldron as cd
import pandas as pd

df: pd.DataFrame = cd.shared.df
```
This variable type annotation lets PyCharm (or other IDE) know that the df variable is a Pandas dataframe. You can do this with older versions of Python as well, but the syntax isn't as elegant:

```
import cauldron as cd
import pandas as pd

df = cd.shared.df  # type: pd.DataFrame
```

This will give you the auto completion you're looking for. It's also a nice way to provide some additional documentation for your code.

The thermo library is built around chemicals with discrete structures. Any mixture of known chemical components can be created, and properties attempted to be calculated for them. There is no theoretical limit to the number of compounds which can be in a mixture, and it is possible to add more chemicals to the database if they are missing. 
Unfortunately, if you don't know the composition you want to represent kerosene with, there is no way to get a property for it. The same goes double for hydraulic fluids - their composition is often proprietary. Because of how many of the correlations and models in thermo work, it is only possible to add chemicals with defined structures.

I would break the loop over the titles when a show_id is found.
Why? Because the show will only be available under 1 `show_id`. If 1 title matches, the others will not match, so no need to look further.

I would still log it as error when no `show_id` could be found at all, just to indicate there is something wrong. 😉 

```python
def list_subtitles(self, video, languages):
        # lookup show_id
        titles = [video.series] + video.alternative_series
        show_id = None
        for title in titles:
            show_id = self.get_show_id(title, video.year)
            if show_id is not None:
                break

        # query for subtitles with the show_id
        if show_id is not None:
            subtitles = [s for s in self.query(show_id, title, video.season, video.year)
                         if s.language in languages and s.episode == video.episode]
            if subtitles:
                return subtitles
        else:
            logger.error('No show id found for %r (%r)', video.series, {'year': video.year})

        return []
```

Incidentally the currently shipped `luma.core-1.7.2-py2.py3-none-any.whl` does not include the `__init__.py` in `luma/` but when I install the whl with `sudo pip install luma.core` and run `import luma.core` Python finds the package happily:

```python
>>> import luma.core
import luma.core # directory /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/core
# trying /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/core/__init__.arm-linux-gnueabihf.so
# trying /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/core/__init__.so

# trying /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/core/__init__.py
# /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/core/__init__.pyc matches /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/core/__init__.py
import luma.core # precompiled from /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/core/__init__.pyc
```

However when I install locally using `sudo python setup.py install` it wont import.

I can only attribute this difference to the `luma.core-1.7.2-py2.7.egg` based install that `setup.py install` produces.

If I build the *new* luma.core (as of my changes) with `python setup.py bdist_wheel` and install the resulting .whl file with pip, then it works.

If I do the same for luma.oled then my `luma` directory in `dist-packages` looks like this:

```
pi@raspberrypi:~ $ ls /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/
core  __init__.py  __init__.pyc  oled
```

And both `import luma.core` and `import luma.oled` continue to work.

*However* if I then uninstall `luma.oled` and reinstall it using `python setup.py install` (with my fix) then `luma.core` will be broken. Fun!

Again, **TLDR**

* Installing *both* `luma.core` and `luma.oled` from .whl files (whether local or via pip) = FINE
* Installing *both* `luma.core` and `luma.oled` with `sudo python setup.py install` (and my fix) = FINE
* Installing each package with a different one of the methods above = BREAKS

And:

Installing *both* packages from .whl files results in them both living in `/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/`

```
pi@raspberrypi:~ $ ls /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/
core  __init__.py  __init__.pyc  oled
```

But when I `sudo pip uninstall luma.oled`

```
pi@raspberrypi:~ $ ls /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/
core
```

*facepalm*

This is with pip 9.0.1. And apparently isn't a new problem: https://github.com/pypa/python-packaging-user-guide/issues/314

This seems to suggest that a `namespace_packages` *is* required, but that results in:

```
pi@raspberrypi:~/luma.oled $ python setup.py build
running build
running build_py
error: Namespace package problem: luma is a namespace package, but its
__init__.py does not call declare_namespace()! Please fix it.
(See the setuptools manual under "Namespace Packages" for details.)
```

So delving into this yields the oh-so-common-of-Python pattern for `luma/__init__.py` of:

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
# Copyright (c) 2014-18 Richard Hull and contributors
# See LICENSE.rst for details.

try:
    from pkg_resources import declare_namespace
    declare_namespace(__name__)
except ImportError:
    from pkgutil import extend_path
    __path__ = extend_path(__path__, __name__)
```

Which results in a `/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/` of:

```
pi@raspberrypi:~/ $ ls /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/
core  oled
```

Dang, that surely wont work, right!??

```python
Python 2.7.13 (default, Nov 24 2017, 17:33:09)
[GCC 6.3.0 20170516] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import luma.oled
>>> import luma.core
>>>
```

Wut!?

Okay let's mess with this: `sudo pip uninstall luma.core` ... `sudo python setup.py install`

```
Python 2.7.13 (default, Nov 24 2017, 17:33:09)
[GCC 6.3.0 20170516] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import luma.core
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: No module named core
>>>
```

!?!?

```
>>> import luma.core
# trying /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/core.arm-linux-gnueabihf.so

# trying /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/core.so
# trying /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/coremodule.so
# trying /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/core.py
# trying /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/core.pyc
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: No module named core
```

Hmm, okay, it never sees the `luma.core-1.7.2-py2.7.egg` because it stops looking after `luma`.

## Summary

The working pattern seems to be to include `namespace_packages=["luma"]` and use an `__init__.py` that looks like:

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
# Copyright (c) 2014-18 Richard Hull and contributors
# See LICENSE.rst for details.

try:
    from pkg_resources import declare_namespace
    declare_namespace(__name__)
except ImportError:
    from pkgutil import extend_path
    __path__ = extend_path(__path__, __name__)
```

This prevents the namespace from being yanked away from installed packages when any namespaced package is uninstalled.

The key caveat of this solution- and it appears *any* solution involving namespacing- is that the packages *must* be installed in the same manner. You can't mix and match .whl installs with .egg installs.

Someone actually went to the trouble of enumerating the working install permutations here: https://github.com/pypa/python-packaging-user-guide/issues/265#issuecomment-276797857

And seems to have a setup for testing this in the repository I was consulting beforehand: https://github.com/pypa/sample-namespace-packages

Sure! You can use a bracket order like so:

```python
instrument.order(
    direction     = "BUY",
    quantity      = 1,
    price         = 100,  # set the price for LMT orders, ommit or set to 0 for MKT orders
    target        = 120,  # brakcet target
    initial_stop  = 90,   # bracket hard stop
    stop_limit    = False # LMT STP / STP stop order
)
```

There are many other parameters you can pass to the `order()` that you should check out here:
http://qtpylib.io/docs/latest/api.html#instrument-api

BTW - if you want to use a bracket order with a trailing stop, use:

```python
instrument.order(
    direction     = "BUY",
    quantity      = 1,
    price         = 100,   # set the price for LMT orders, ommit or set to 0 for MKT orders
    target        = 120,   # brakcet target
    initial_stop  = 90,    # bracket hard stop
    stop_limit    = False, # LMT STP / STP stop order
    trail_stop_at = 110,   # start trailing when price reaches this level
    trail_stop_by = 5,     # % of trailing stop distance from current price
)
```

Or, if you want to control the stoploss manually, use:

```python
instrument.move_stoploss(110) # 110 is the new stop loss now
```

You can also use a shorthand for a buy MKT order (and ommit the `directin` and `price` parametrs)

```python
instrument.buy(quantity=1, ... )
```

...or a buy LMT order

```python
instrument.buy_limit(quantity=1, price=100, ... )
```

Same goes for `instrument.sell` and `instrument.sell_limit` :)

I really recommend familiarizing yourself with the [Instrument API](http://qtpylib.io/docs/latest/api.html#instrument-api), as there are some powerful/helpful stuff :)

One example, you can get the maximum number of Futures contracts you're allowed to to trade, based on your margin, buy calling:

```python
# for overnight holding
max_allowed = instrument.get_max_contracts_allowed(overnight=True)

# for intraday holding
max_allowed = instrument.get_max_contracts_allowed(overnight=False)
```

Live long and prosper, my friend :)

@mementum	Rework Resampling and address comment in #24 to avoid last bar carryi… …	c2b66eb

(The above message copied from issue 24 due to a typo in the commit message)

Resampling has been reworked to avoid the corner case above in which the last bar still carried the timestamp and offer further control with parameters

    Params

      - bar2edge (default: True)

        resamples using time boundaries as the target. For example with a
        "ticks -> 5 seconds" the resulting 5 seconds bars will be aligned to
        xx:00, xx:05, xx:10 ...

      - adjbartime (default: True)

        Use the time at the boundary to adjust the time of the delivered
        resampled bar instead of the last seen timestamp. If resampling to "5
        seconds" the time of the bar will be adjusted for example to hh:mm:05 even
        if the last seen timestamp was hh:mm:04.33

        .. note::

           Time will only be adjusted if "bar2edge" is True. It wouldn't make
           sense to adjust the time if the bar has not been aligned to a
           boundary

      - rightedge (default: False)

        Use the right edge of the time boundaries to set the time.

        If False and compressing to 5 seconds the time of a resampled bar for
        seconds between hh:mm:00 and hh:mm:04 will be hh:mm:00 (the starting
        boundary

        If True the used boundary for the time will be hh:mm:05 (the ending
        boundary)

This slightly alters the existing behavior shown in the Tick Data Resampling post. The sample has been updated and the entire post will also be updated.

Barring further problems/comments with any testing, this will be put in new release together with the fix for #24

> Is your concern mainly about performance, or something else?

It's about correctness. Betamax has a few record modes (specifically `all`) that would do what it sounds like you want, *but*, you need to close the connection after each request/set of requests to ensure that it's actually written to disk. And if you record everything, that will not playback anything. Having Betamax between your session and your service won't cause issues but closing/opening cassettes means that Betamax *will* **try** to replay the existing interactions before recording new ones. That means you might have made a similar request an hour earlier and be making it again and you'll get old data instead of the newest/correct data.

Keep in mind that serializing/deserializing large amounts of JSON is also not fast. You'd probably want to write a custom serializer class for Betamax that uses a better serialization format.

If you want to cache things, I'd strongly suggest you look at CacheControl but it sounds like you also want to record exception responses to be able to test for them and then build workarounds?

> Would thet be possible?

I don't know how to do that, but it *might* be possible. If you can research that and send a pull request, I would happily review it.

# Forward propagation
def forward_prop(params):
    # Neural network architecture
    n_inputs = X_train.shape[1]
    n_hidden = 20
    n_classes = 1

    # Roll-back the weights and biases
    W1 = params[0:80].reshape((n_inputs,n_hidden))
    b1 = params[80:100].reshape((n_hidden,))
    W2 = params[100:120].reshape((n_hidden,n_classes))
    b2 = params[120:121].reshape((n_classes,))

    # Perform forward propagation

    z2 = a1.dot(W2) + b2 # Pre-activation in Layer 2
    logits = z2          # Logits for Layer 2

    # Compute for the softmax of the logits
    exp_scores = np.exp(logits)
    probs = exp_scores / np.sum(exp_scores, axis=1, keepdims=True)

    # Compute for the negative log likelihood

We used gplearn as a baseline representation of symbolic regression to compare our methods to. gplearn didn't do very well compared to XGBoost and our methods, although it did beat Lasso regression on most of the benchmark datasets when given enough population size & generations (1000x1000)

I have also added a check at each evolution to see whether older generations are still relevant, ie whether any of their "dna" exists in the current generation. Any irrelevant programs will be removed from the old generation's population by marking them as `None`. This results in a massive reduction of the number of programs stored and should help significantly with memory use.

class JSONEncoder(json.JSONEncoder):
    def default(self, obj):
        if hasattr(obj, '__json__'):
            return obj.__json__()
        elif isinstance(obj, collections.Iterable):
            return list(obj)
        elif isinstance(obj, dt.datetime):
            return obj.isoformat()
        elif hasattr(obj, '__getitem__') and hasattr(obj, 'keys'):
            return dict(obj)
        elif hasattr(obj, '__dict__'):

                    for member in dir(obj)

from __future__ import division
import random
import requests
import datetime as dt
import json
from functools import partial
from multiprocessing.pool import Pool

from twitterscraper.tweet import Tweet
from twitterscraper.logging import logger


**ua1 = "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 6.1; WOW64; rv:40.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/40.1"
ua2 = "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 6.1) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/41.0.2228.0 Safari/537.36"
ua3 = "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows; U; Windows NT 6.1; x64; fr; rv:1.9.2.13) Gecko/20101203 Firebird/3.6.13"
ua4 = "Mozilla/5.0 (compatible, MSIE 11, Windows NT 6.3; Trident/7.0; rv:11.0) like Gecko"
ua5 = "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows; U; Windows NT 6.1; rv:2.2) Gecko/20110201"
ua6 = "Opera/9.80 (X11; Linux i686; Ubuntu/14.10) Presto/2.12.388 Version/12.16"
ua7 = "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 5.2; RW; rv:7.0a1) Gecko/20091211 SeaMonkey/9.23a1pre"
HEADERS_LIST = [ua1, ua2, ua3, ua4, ua5, ua6, ua7]**

INIT_URL = "https://twitter.com/search?f=tweets&vertical=default&q=**from:**{q}&l={lang}"
RELOAD_URL = "https://twitter.com/i/search/timeline?f=tweets&vertical=" \
             "default&include_available_features=1&include_entities=1&" \
             "reset_error_state=false&src=typd&max_position={pos}&q=**from:**{q}&l={lang}"

**def linspace(start, stop, n):
    if n == 1:
        yield stop
        return
    h = (stop - start) / (n - 1)
    for i in range(n):
        yield start + h * i**


def query_single_page(url, html_response=True, retry=10):
    """
    Returns tweets from the given URL.
    :param url: The URL to get the tweets from
    :param html_response: False, if the HTML is embedded in a JSON
    :param retry: Number of retries if something goes wrong.
    :return: The list of tweets, the pos argument for getting the next page.
    """
    headers = {'User-Agent': random.choice(HEADERS_LIST)}

    try:
        response = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
        if html_response:
            html = response.text or ''
        else:
            html = ''
            try:
                json_resp = json.loads(response.text)
                html = json_resp['items_html'] or ''
            except ValueError as e:
                logger.exception('Failed to parse JSON "{}" while requesting "{}"'.format(e, url))

        tweets = list(Tweet.from_html(html))

        if not tweets:
            return [], None

        if not html_response:
            return tweets, json_resp['min_position']

        return tweets, "TWEET-{}-{}".format(tweets[-1].id, tweets[0].id)
    except requests.exceptions.HTTPError as e:
        logger.exception('HTTPError {} while requesting "{}"'.format(
            e, url))
    except requests.exceptions.ConnectionError as e:
        logger.exception('ConnectionError {} while requesting "{}"'.format(
            e, url))
    except requests.exceptions.Timeout as e:
        logger.exception('TimeOut {} while requesting "{}"'.format(
            e, url))
    except json.decoder.JSONDecodeError as e:
        logger.exception('Failed to parse JSON "{}" while requesting "{}".'.format(
            e, url))

    if retry > 0:
        logger.info("Retrying... (Attempts left: {})".format(retry))
        return query_single_page(url, html_response, retry-1)

    logger.error("Giving up.")
    return [], None


def query_tweets_once(query, limit=None, lang=''):
    """
    Queries twitter for all the tweets you want! It will load all pages it gets
    from twitter. However, twitter might out of a sudden stop serving new pages,
    in that case, use the `query_tweets` method.
    Note that this function catches the KeyboardInterrupt so it can return
    tweets on incomplete queries if the user decides to abort.
    :param query: Any advanced query you want to do! Compile it at
                  https://twitter.com/search-advanced and just copy the query!
    :param limit: Scraping will be stopped when at least ``limit`` number of
                  items are fetched.
    :param num_tweets: Number of tweets fetched outside this function.
    :return:      A list of twitterscraper.Tweet objects. You will get at least
                  ``limit`` number of items.
    """
    logger.info("Querying {}".format(query))
    query = query.replace(' ', '%20').replace("#", "%23").replace(":", "%3A")
    pos = None
    tweets = []
    try:
        while True:
            new_tweets, pos = query_single_page(
                INIT_URL.format(q=query, lang=lang) if pos is None
                else RELOAD_URL.format(q=query, pos=pos, lang=lang),
                pos is None
            )
            if len(new_tweets) == 0:
                logger.info("Got {} tweets for {}.".format(
                    len(tweets), query))
                return tweets

            tweets += new_tweets

            if limit and len(tweets) >= limit:
                logger.info("Got {} tweets for {}.".format(
                    len(tweets), query))
                return tweets
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        logger.info("Program interrupted by user. Returning tweets gathered "
                     "so far...")
    except BaseException:
        logger.exception("An unknown error occurred! Returning tweets "
                          "gathered so far.")
    logger.info("Got {} tweets for {}.".format(
        len(tweets), query))
    return tweets


def eliminate_duplicates(iterable):
    """
    Yields all unique elements of an iterable sorted. Elements are considered
    non unique if the equality comparison to another element is true. (In those
    cases, the set conversion isn't sufficient as it uses identity comparison.)
    """
    class NoElement: pass

    prev_elem = NoElement
    for elem in sorted(iterable):
        if prev_elem is NoElement:
            prev_elem = elem
            yield elem
            continue

        if prev_elem != elem:
            prev_elem = elem
            yield elem

def query_tweets(query, limit=None, begindate=dt.date(2006,3,21), enddate=dt.date.today(), poolsize=20, lang=''):
    no_days = (enddate - begindate).days
    if poolsize > no_days:
        # Since we are assigning each pool a range of dates to query,
		# the number of pools should not exceed the number of dates.
        poolsize = no_days
    dateranges = [begindate + dt.timedelta(days=elem) for elem in linspace(0, no_days, poolsize+1)]

    if limit:
        limit_per_pool = (limit // poolsize)+1
    else:
        limit_per_pool = None

    queries = ['{} since:{} until:{}'.format(query, since, until)
               for since, until in zip(dateranges[:-1], dateranges[1:])]

    all_tweets = []
    try:
        pool = Pool(poolsize)

        try:
            i=0
            for new_tweets in pool.imap_unordered(partial(query_tweets_once, limit=limit_per_pool, lang=lang), queries):
                all_tweets.extend(new_tweets)
                i=i+1
                logger.info("{}: Got {} tweets ({} new).".format(i,
                    len(all_tweets), len(new_tweets)))
        except KeyboardInterrupt:
            logger.info("Program interrupted by user. Returning all tweets "
                         "gathered so far.")
    finally:
        pool.close()
        pool.join()

    return all_tweets

Hi Guy

Thanks for your report.

I think the second print statement in your script would have to be changed to:
``` python
>>>print(", ".join(blob.words.lemmatize()))
das, Auto, sein, sehr, schön
```
The output you've described is not a bug but standard behaviour on Python2 (Python3 would give you the expected output).

**EXPLANATION:**  Your second output line is the standard representation of non-ASCII characters in **Python2** data structures and not a bug or botched up characters:

``` python
>>>a = [u"schön"]
>>>print(a)
[u'sch\xf6n']
# The umlaut is displayed correctly when the
# string within the list is printed:
>>>print(a[0])
schön
```

`blob.words.lemmatize()` returns a list (consistent with `textblob` main package):
``` python
>>>blob.words.lemmatize()
WordList([u'das', u'Auto', u'sein', u'sehr', u'sch\xf6n'])
>>>print(blob.words.lemmatize())
[u'das', u'Auto', u'sein', u'sehr', u'sch\xf6n']
```

You can either iterate over this list to get the string representations:
``` python
>>>for lemma in blob.words.lemmatize():
           print(lemma)
das
Auto
sein
sehr
schön
```

Or you could use the following statement to print the list of lemmas as one-line string, as suggested above:

``` python
>>>print(", ".join(blob.words.lemmatize()))
das, Auto, sein, sehr, schön
```

3. `%h = "a"` + `%g = "b/c"`

Please see #89. This is the same issue.

The issue is that the Windows conda package for `dlib` doesn't register itself in a way that `pip` can see, so it tries to re-install another copy of `dlib` since it thinks it is missing. 

You can work around this by installing `face_recognition` without automatically installing it's dependencies:

```
pip install --no-dependencies face_recognition face_recognition_models
```

But keep in mind that no dependencies at all are installed this way. So if you run into any errors about any missing libraries besides `dlib`, you might need to manually install those libraries with `pip install library_name` as well.

I have never used ROS and am not familiar with it in particular, so I don't know for sure.

But face_recognition itself is purely python and has no CMake component to compile. Nothing with CMake would be required. Only dlib (which this depends on) uses CMake.

Based on the error you got, one obvious thing to check is to make sure you didn't name your own file `face_recogntion.py` because that is one thing that could cause the error you saw since that name would conflict with the name of the library itself and make Python load your file again instead of loading the real library.

Beyond that, I'm not sure because I've never seen ROS.

I'm late to this, but this is caused by a difference in what Yahoo calls Adjusted Close on their website and on their data sets. On their website, adjusted close only accounts for stock splits, whereas on their data sets it adjusts for both stock splits and dividends.

You can see this because the first discrepancy in the AAPL data is on the 8th of February, which is when they emitted a dividend. The dividend was 0.57 per share, which was a return of 0.57/132.04=0.0043. If you look at the adjusted close for the day, it is equal to the closing price (and adjusted close on the website)*(1-0.0043). Google Finance also does not adjust for dividends. 
I attached a screenshot with all 4 data sources for illustration.
![screenshot 40](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/20395965/23579624/edd77246-00e8-11e7-8e12-182fcbbaaadf.png)

Yeah, let me try to illustrate it better with an example.

Inside our `process` call it goes a little like this currently:

```
    def process(self):
        """
        The business logic of creating a new loan.
        :return:
        """
        try:

            api = ExternalAPI()
            # The next step creates a "loan" instance with an external service
            status, loan_instance = api.loans.create(loan_data)

        except Exception:

            # This effectively withdraws the externally-stored loan instance and
            # keeps a record of this withdrawal in the local database.
            # Critically, because of the atomic transaction this local record would be undone!
            if loan_instance:
                transaction = LoanTransaction(loan=loan_instance)
                transaction.create_withdraw()
                api.loans.withdraw(loan_instance)

            raise
```

In a nutshell, while it's sometimes handy to have the atomic transaction, I don't think something as neutral and basic as a `Service` class should assume that we always have database events that we want to wrap into a transaction.

Keen to hear your view!

one idea is to make a generic filter which adds annotations within a span for a scoped operation..

```
before: Annotation <- added at the beginning of a task
after: Annotation <- added unconditionally after a task
after_failure: Annotation + ": $error.class: $error.message" <- additionally added when the task failed
```

ex. I could add do something like (forgive the quasi code)
```
start_new_span
  filter("cs", "cr", "Client Send Error: $error.class: $error.message")
    httpclient.get
        filter("ws", "wr", "Wire Send Error: $error.class: $error.message")
          http_transport.send
```

In this case, the span above would include in normal case: ["cs", "ws", "wr", "cr"]

This is the design finagle uses, and elaborated here
https://github.com/openzipkin/openzipkin.github.io/issues/52

Are you sure boto3 is causing the issue? The multiprocessing library actually doesn't use threads but spawns sub-processes. This is different from threads in that the sub-processes have their own memory space and thus sharing variables between them is more complicated than when using threads. Thanks for the pull request.  From our [contributing guidelines](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/CONTRIBUTING.rst) these files are generated upstream so we typically don't accept modifications directly to these files.

This is affecting our organization as well so I would love to have a way to manually adjust the connection pool size. We're occasionally exceeding the limit because our multi-threaded web server can sometimes make > 10 requests to S3 in parallel.

When this happens we start getting "Connection pool is full, discarding connection" errors (from urllib3, which must be used under the hood by boto). I believe everything continues to function fine, because we aren't hitting any hard limit merely overflowing the pool, but our application would be more efficient (and less noisy) if we could adjust the pool size.

I'd be open to any suggested workarounds, too. The `phet_roh` represents the probability of observing a het in a run of homozygosity. This a function (crudely the product of) de novo mutation rate and sequencing error rate. For Anopheles (our main use case) the de novo rate is low, and the error rate due to misalignments etc is relatively high. 

(I'll submit a PR to make this clearer!)

As the background rate of diveristy in non ROH regions is so high, the model is usually easily able to distinguish between ROH/non ROH. However, where the background diversity is lower, parameter choice may be more of an issue. 

We have run BCFtools, and recover good agreement. Although this hasn't been done extensively, or over a broad range of parameters. We would be very interested if you choose to do this. Happy to share these analyses if of interest.

A significant advantage of this method over BCF tools is that we can account for inaccessible regions of the genome. BCF tools tends to call these as ROH as they look like low diversity regions as VCF file has no entries for them.

Like most packaging tools, pipenv depends upon you having pip and setuptools. As a consequence it also doesn’t allow you to alter which versions of these things you have in your environment using pipenv itself. If pipenv were able to touch these or allowed you to do that, it would be very easy to destroy your environment. I appreciate that in this specific use case of having an outdated artifactory in a distributed system where you don’t want to tell people to run a manual step, it would be more convenient, but in most other cases the behavior as designed is desirable.

We made a conscious choice not to lift short arguments from Xplat. As you point out, for one command, `vnet-name` is `-e` and another it is `-m`.  Neither makes any sense. The post_check function does not really need that information since you already can know about it as a user. The only thing you cannot know is the output_path which is why it is provided. It is all quite easy in Python, consider the following template using nested functions to create a test case with pre_config and post_check functions:
```python
def create_config(name, test_bench, my_test_parameters):
    generics = f(my_test_parameters)
    
    def pre_config(output_path):
        # I can use my_test_parameters or generics here

    def post_check(output_path):
        # I can use my_test_parameters or generics here

    test_bench.add_config(name, generics=generics, post_check=post_check, pre_config=pre_config)

It is also possible to use classes and objects to keep the information as a user.
```

As you say, there are lots of ways this could be done.  I have a few comments / suggestions.

* `nucleotideOrAa` is a bad function name. It would be ok if it returned a string like `nt` or `aa` but if it returns a Boolean, anyone reading code that uses this function will not be able to tell what the function returns.  `if nucleotideOrAa(read) ...` is completely uninformative. Instead you should use a name that says something, like `isNucleotides` or `isAA` or something.
* The function shouldn't warn. Returning a Boolean is enough. Let the caller can decide what to do - exit, warn, raise, etc.
* It would help to think about the context in which this issue was raised. I used `create-database.py` and passed a file of nt data. How are we going to detect that? What other `bin` scripts should check their arguments? `find-matches.py`. Both those scripts end up using `combineReads` in `fasta.py`, so maybe something in there should be checking.
* When checking a sequence, it's probably enough to just read the first (say) 10 residues and if they're all in ACGT to conclude that the sequence is nucleotides. You're doing a probabilistic job anyway, so not much is lost. The (naive) probability that an AA sequence starts with 10 residues from ACGT is about 1 in 10 million. The reason I suggest this is that there's really nothing to be gained from checking the whole read sequence - it's just slower. Probably you can tell if a sequence is not nucleotides after just a couple of residues.
* Similarly, it's probably enough just to look at a couple of reads in a FASTA file to detect if it's nucleotides.

I don't know the right answer here. But I think I'd do the following:

* Change your function to have a more informative name.
* In `combineReads` where it looks at all the manually added reads "Add any individually specified AA subject sequences." I'd check each read. These have all come from the command line, so there wont be many and each one is error prone.
* In `FastaReads` in the `iter` function I would have it check the first few sequences to make sure they're not nucleotide. Something like this (untested):

```python
    def iter(self):
        """
        Iterate over the sequences in self.file_, yielding each as an
        instance of the desired read class. Check the first few sequences to
        make sure they appear to be of the expected type (nucleotide vs amino
        acid).
        """
        dnaReads = self.readClass is DNARead

        for count, seq in enumerate(SeqIO.parse(self.file_, 'fasta'), start=1):
            read = self.readClass(seq.description, str(seq.seq))
            if count < 6:
                if read.isDNA():
                    if not dnaReads:
                        raise RuntimeError('DNA sequence found on line %d '
                                           'of %r.' % (count, self.file_))
                else:
                    if dnaReads:
                        raise RuntimeError('Non-DNA sequence found on line %d '
                                           'of %r.' % (count, self.file_))
            yield read
```

Thanks for this! For the lazy future me / other people who run into this and don't want to read the whole thread, the solution is to symlink your local environment into the global envs/ directory in the anaconda install path.

The tricky behavior is that it looks like the environment correctly activated, which is technically true, because activation doesn't really check or do anything. Our use case is that we install conda environments into non-local shared directories because they get pretty huge, and often overflow the per-user space quotas we have.  Just better documenting "install an env to a specific path" would go a long way.

The advantage of not requiring an environment variable is that it "just works". No need to source the activate script or put something in your bash profile.

Another thing to be aware of here... Conda 4.4 lifted the restriction that conda could only be installed in the base environment.  That restriction came about because of the symlinking behavior on activate, and now that we're not doing that, we also don't need the restriction.  Since there's been a strong driver for conda to be both an *application* and a *library* (with the push to have stable internal APIs), lifting the restriction makes sense.

As a future improvement, it might make sense for conda *not* to include the `bin/conda` entry point when conda isn't installed into the base environment, to further enforce it's use as a library in non-base envs.

We already have some "magic" happening in [plone.protect transforms](https://github.com/plone/plone.protect/blob/e156eb1d7e81b99a81ea048ef79bd58da55ca73b/plone/protect/auto.py#L263), automatically adding tokens to our forms. The idea is to have this same ease of use but now in AJAX calls.

Imagine you have a Plone 4 addon, using plone4.csrffixes. You have 50 ajax calls. You want to make it compatible with Plone 5. You have two options:

- Add _authenticator variables across all of your ajax calls, getting it's value from an input in a form;
- Copy protect.js from plone4.csrffixes.

Our opinion is: if you don't need to explicitly add tokens to forms, you shouldn't need this in AJAX calls either. This is consistent with plone.protect README as well:

> Automatic CSRF Protection

> Since version 3, plone.protect **provides automatic CSRF protection**. It does this by automatically including the auth token to all internal forms when the user requesting the page is logged in.

Good, question.

The short answer: overkill

I think that these two templates (email text and email html) don't justify the complexity of setting up jinja (prior to django 1.8) and adding a dependency as big as jinja.

django (as with jinja) is too permissive and would open the door to lots of crazy stuff being done in those templates, so to avoid all these I decided to keep it as simple as possible and just use python's builtin string template, I thought about using something like pystache but it wasn't justified.

I'm open to suggestion though if you think there could be a scenario where logical operators are needed in these.

@manthey plugins like the girder_io  or docker plugin are really more core "application plugins"  they extend the core functionality of girder_worker (e.g. functionality implemented by girder_worker.run,  girder_worker.validate, etc).  This PR introduces "task plugins" which allows separate pip-installable packages to _add too or completely replace_ the core girder_worker functionality by introducing new celery tasks.

 If you include the core tasks (which is the default)  all of the application plugins will be loaded as before.

Ok, upon second look I think I spot the issue. When you bind pybind functions involving non-reference/pointer arguments, a copy of the argument made internally for tricky implementation reasons. Your class doesn't allow this, thus the compilation error. Use pass-by-reference or pointer semantics to avoid this.

That's neat -- however, yet one more alternative would be to include the more elegant ``overload_cast`` syntax and simply make this a C++14 only API. This will become a non-issue shortly (actually, if it works on Visual studio, I'd say that it is basically already a non-issue).

Wow, so the connection pooling feature of the Requests library is quite confusing. It's not well-documented, but this issue [1] helps us understand that we do need an exterior session to get connection pooling work, but as this issue [2] indicates, you can actually pool connections across sessions as well because it's all just backed by the underlying library.

It seems to me that the right thing to do here might be to just create a session object internal to `HTTPClient` and just use that for every single request. It doesn't even have to leak out to `APIRequestor`. Thoughts @NahomAgidew?

As I understand it, the root issue is that Theano calls `setup` with `version='0.7.0'` even in the development version, so pip cannot distinguish between a git version and a previously installed release.

I think the real solution would be for Theano to adopt something like [Versioneer](https://github.com/warner/python-versioneer). But we could also switch to telling people to do

`pip install --upgrade git+https://github.com/Theano/Theano.git@15c90dd3`

This will still display the warning `Could not find a tag or branch '15c90dd3', assuming commit.`, but that's a bit less disconcerting than the version mismatch warning.

For me, at least, this *definitely* seems related to whether the From email uses a sender domain that's authenticated with SendGrid (https://app.sendgrid.com/settings/sender_auth). If the From domain is not authenticated, the "smtp-id" sent to the webhooks is *different* from the Message-ID header in the sent/received message.

Details...

When I send messages from an authenticated, DKIM-validated domain in my SG account...

```http
From: from@test-sg.anymail.info
Message-ID: <152712480034.85680.5099186793774837923@test-sg.anymail.info>
```
... the webhook payload has the correct smtp-id:
```json
  "smtp-id": "<152712480034.85680.5099186793774837923@test-sg.anymail.info>"
```

BUT when I send messages from @example.com, which is (obviously) *not* an authenticated domain for my SG account, I see exactly the behavior you described: the received message's Message-ID header matches what Anymail sent...
```http
From: from@example.com
Message-ID: <152712433591.85282.8340115595767222398@example.com>
```
... but the webhook payloads have a totally different smtp-id:
```json
  "smtp-id": "<-g6-N0riQ3WNhvp9aBJpfQ@ismtpd0006p1sjc2.sendgrid.net>
```

Please let me know what you hear back from SendGrid support. (I could sort of understand if they were trying to prevent un-authenticated sender domains from appearing anywhere in sent mail headers, but since they're leaving @example.com in the Message-ID and the From header, that's not what's going on.)

If anyone from SendGrid engineering stops by here while investigating, the examples above are not redacted; feel free to pull them up in your logs. Also, that incorrect "smtp-id" from the from@example.com webhook payload actually does appear in the message headers, as an "HTTP id":

I did some testing with this and have something that works but it has edge cases where things that matched before wont match now because it skips the next occurrence for the next occurrence on a boundary.

```
describe-classic-link-instances
^--^------^---^
1  2      3   4
```
This completion is currently punished in two ways:
1) It's skipping to characters that aren't at a 'word' boundary (moves 2,3,4).
2) It's not matching most of the completion string (15/31 characters)

Compared to:
```
describe-reserved-instances-listings
^--^------------------------^^
1  2                        34
```
Completes 30/36. And the jump from 2 -> 3 isn't penalized very much because 3 is at a boundary. So it basically gets to skip two words and most of the string for nearly free.

Where it's possible for `dcli` to match like this:
```
describe-classic-link-instances
^--------^-------^----^
1        2       3    4
```
In this case you complete more of the word and the moves are to word boundaries (specifically the *next* word boundary) which isn't penalized as much. We would need to add some prioritization for the next occurrence of the character vs the next occurrence of the character at a boundary or test both and choose the 'better' completion score.

To bring this issue up to date, here's what still needs to be done:

1. We need to set `BOOTSTRAP_VERSION` [here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/5f6b1378eca5a157a35f36e778e8b26702d758d0/certbot-auto#L717) to a different value only for those we're going to force to Python 3. Changing this value will cause `certbot-auto` to (re)invoke the user's package manager or print a message that we need the user to run `certbot-auto` interactively to upgrade so we only want to change this value on the systems we have to.
2. In the same place we set `BOOTSTRAP_VERSION`, we need to set `Bootstrap` to a function that can be run to install OS dependencies on CentOS/RHEL 6 including Python 3. This should probably be done by creating a new [bootstrap script](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/tree/5f6b1378eca5a157a35f36e778e8b26702d758d0/letsencrypt-auto-source/pieces/bootstrappers) and refactor `rpm_common.sh` in that directory to allow this script to reuse common code.
3. Ensure Python 3 is used when creating the virtualenv. This should be doable by setting the environment variable `LE_PYTHON` to the name of the Python 3 executable in the user's PATH and exporting it.
4. Profit?

Ideally, the sysadmin doesn't need to keep track of this. If Certbot is set up for automatic renewal, the certificates will renewed before they expire. If Certbot is unable to renew these certificates for some reason, it will exit with an error code and more information about what went wrong which should cause a notification to be sent to a sysadmin if root e-mail is properly set up (which I know it often isn't).

Additionally, Let's Encrypt sends you e-mail if your certificate has not been renewed and it is approaching expiration. I believe it does this 3 weeks before expiration and then again 1 week before. With this setup, if you ever get an e-mail from Let's Encrypt about expired certificates, you know there might be a problem.

If these notifications aren't enough for your use case, we could consider adding this, however, it'll probably be a wishlist ticket until other issues have been resolved.

Awesome. There's actually a decent amount of documentation already written, but it's spread around throughout our repo and needs to be cleaned up. Take a look at the comments in #3521 as well as the output from `certbot --help manual` from `master`.

For the most part, everything is as Joona describes in #3521. The only real change is the script plugin was merged into the manual plugin. References to `--script` should be changed to `--manual`, `--script-auth` to `--manual-auth-hook`, and `--script-cleanup` to `--manual-cleanup-hook`.

Basically, the way the manual plugin works now is if the user provides a shell command or a path to an executable with `--manual-auth-hook`, the manual plugin behaves like the script plugin Joona describes. If this flag is not provided, the manual plugin works like it always did; simply giving the user instructions on how to set up their system to pass the challenge from the ACME CA themselves.

We should explain these two use cases in the style we do for [our other plugins](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#plugins).

I think there's a lot of nice things we could do for explaining the use case with hooks, however, some might be a bit tricky and I don't think they are strictly necessary. Ideally, for the use case with hooks, we should include an example that other people can use to as a starting point and to build their own scripts off of. While more complicated, I think doing a DNS example here makes the most sense as this how I expect most people would be using this script. Taking it a step further, it'd be nice if this example was for a real API like Cloudflare, Namecheap, etc. This gives people a real example to work with and allows users of those services to simply copy and paste the script we provide in our documentation.

Curator requires http access as it is API-driven.  It does not need to be run only on the masters, or anything like that.  It only needs to run against a single client, which can be any node at all, provided you haven't disabled http access.

Thanks @takluyver and @ssanderson . 

Forgive me for the `not not`. 

It was not my intention to promote it - its just that not being familiar with python or its type-conversion system, I used the `not not` merely to demonstrate the concept. It was just one of the old techniques from the days of x86 assembly / C to force-cast the objects to bools. 

But I see from your messages that python has this `bool()` to take care of the conversion - in which case it is better to stick with `bool()`. 

As for the naming, I have no preference either. Whichever works for you, is fine with me. But as @takluyver pointed, the semantics certainly need to be clear for the user. For example, something on the lines of `has_checkpoints()` is also not far from the concept.

BTW, does python support polymorphic functions (where multiple methods can have same function name with different parameter types). In that case multiple methods with same name such as below may satisfy @takluyver 's criteria:
````
has_checkpoints(for_this_path) :
     #some code

has_checkpoints(for_this_id):
    # some code

has_checkpoints(for_this_condition):
    # some code

````

If not, what is the typical way Python achieves the above kind of mechanism?

One simple way would be - if the `path`, `id` etc. were objects themselves on which we can apply `has_checkpoints'. For example,
````
    def has_checkpoints(pathString):
          return PATH_Class(pathString).has_checkpoints();
````
where `PATH_Class` is a light-weight wrapper that creates a `path` object that has specialized `has_checkpoints()` defined on it from the user supplied `pathString`. 

The `PATH_Class` then be defined as a Class Trait ( mixin in pyton?) for the checkpoint class.

Yeah, I'm happy to add `Patch.data`, as well as a read-only buffer protocol interface. The latter could be helpful when dealing with very large diffs, similar to very large blobs. Buffers probably make less sense for DiffLine.

Though, would `.text` be hardwired to replace invalid code sequences (`errors='replace'`, like Patch.patch does now)? If we do that, making it seemingly trivial to convert between bytes and text, then I worry that we might to continue to feed into the [common misunderstandings](https://github.com/libgit2/pygit2/issues/610#issuecomment-383665869) around text decoding.

I defined `.encode()` to default to `errors='strict'` so as to blow up on non-UTF8 data. A method or attribute that silently truncates its input doesn't seem attractive.

Do we really need a shorthand for decoding a patch/blob/line into text? With Git's lack of encoding information, this is an inherently tricky and unreliable proposition.

I feel that a generic run-over-SSH feature is very useful. 

For example, now I have a workflow whose data sources are scattered on different clusters: taskA needs to access HDFS files on one cluster A, taskB needs to access Hive tables on another cluster B.

One problem is that now it's not easy to run taskA and taskB in the same workflow. If I start the workflow on cluster A, then taskB can not use hive related tasks/targets in the luigi package. If I start on B, then I can not use HdfsTarget. 

Another problem with this is that I need to install luigi on both clusters. Because clusters are managed, I can not install it globally. Instead, I installed it for the accounts that run the pipeline. This means I need to install luigi for diff accounts (testing, staging, prod) on both clusters. 

The example is simple and there are certainly ways to overcome the problems using what luigi have (for example, use external command task).  But it's not generic and elegant. If we could have a solution to run all luigi Tasks over SSH, it will be great.

Hope I made the point!

Thanks for the clarification.  Currently we are set up for cookie authentication only when using the `connect(...)` method on a client.  But fortunately with Python there is usually a workaround.  For this case the work around to use Basic authentication you basically would need to "roll your own" connection routine as in the following:

```python
from cloudant import Cloudant
import requests

# Construct a client object but don't provide it with your password.
# However, the password (auth_token) argument must be set to something.
client = Cloudant('bradwbonn', 'not-giving-you-my-password', account='bradwbonn')
# Roll your own connect() routine...
client.r_session = requests.Session()
client.r_session.headers.update({'Authorization': 'Basic YWxmaW5rZWwyOmxlYjA1d3NraQ=='})
client._cloudant_session = c.session()
# Done ...
```

Unfortunately, you cannot use the Cloudant *with* context manager using this work around as in your original example.  Also, if you were using the `url` kwarg along with the `x_cloudant_user` kwarg you would need to account for an additional `User-Agent` header but you get the gist...

I hope that works out for you.

I'll open a new "Enhancement" Issue for adding Basic authentication and reference this one.

I couldn't take the "slightly easier" approach in my case and went instead for the "replace the pool manager" version.

The steps I took:
* Create a pool manager with a different behaviour.
* Create a requests HTTP adapter that uses a that pool manager.
* Mount it in the session.

Seems to work nicely. I'll leave it below, for posterity.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import logging
import urlparse

import requests


class PerFullURLPoolManager(requests.packages.urllib3.poolmanager.PoolManager):

    def connection_from_url(self, url):
        # Adapted from PoolManager.connection_from_host.
        # Difference: pool_key=url instead of (scheme, host, port)
        if not url:
            raise LocationValueError("No url specified.")
        pool_key = url
        with self.pools.lock:
            pool = self.pools.get(pool_key)
            if pool:
                return pool
            up = urlparse.urlparse(url)
            pool = self._new_pool(up.scheme, up.hostname, up.port)
            self.pools[pool_key] = pool
        return pool


class PerFullURLHTTPAdapter(requests.adapters.HTTPAdapter):

    def init_poolmanager(self, connections, maxsize, block=False, **pool_kwargs):
        # Adapted from HTTPAdapter.init_poolmanager.
        # Difference: poolmanager class.
        self._pool_connections = connections
        self._pool_maxsize = maxsize
        self._pool_block = block
        self.poolmanager = PerFullURLPoolManager(
                                num_pools=connections,
                                maxsize=maxsize,
                                block=block,
                                strict=True,
                                **pool_kwargs
                            )


if __name__ == '__main__':

    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)

    s = requests.Session()

    # Use a different connection pool per URL.
    # Comment these out and connections are pooled on a scheme/host/port basis.
    a = PerFullURLHTTPAdapter()
    s.mount('http://', a)
    s.mount('https://', a)

    # Will generate six connections with PerFullURLHTTPAdapter.
    # Or a single one with the default HTTPAdapter.
    s.get('http://127.0.0.1:8080/a')
    s.get('http://127.0.0.1:8080/b')
    s.get('http://127.0.0.1:8080/a?qa1')
    s.get('http://127.0.0.1:8080/b?qb1')
    s.get('http://127.0.0.1:8080/a?qa2')
    s.get('http://127.0.0.1:8080/b?qb2')
```

I don't have the time at the moment to very closely read this, but I think there are a lot of fundamental flaws in this pull request.

For example, operations on the original request shouldn't be done via `response.request` if we have to do anything like that, it should not be a constant access via the response object because attribute access hurts in Python more than people realize. Further, I think we support a set of requests versions that means we can *not* assume that a `PreparedRequest` has a `copy` method. Admittedly, there isn't automated CI for this project, but that doesn't mean we can just break backwards compatibility randomly. I'm also confused as to why we're releasing the socket but then using `response.connection` (which if memory serves me right is the `urllib3.HTTPConnection` (which should have said socket). In real environment, asymmetric keys are used and signer and verifier are generally difference actors, signer have private key and verifier have only public key corresponding to signer's private key.  PEM encoded private key is suitable for signer side in terms of operational ease, on the other hand JWK(JSON) format is convenience to distribute publicly.

So I presented two ways in README to express python-jwt is supported both sides.

Code is trying to pull /client/v4/certificates/[ZONE_ID] when it should be pulling ?zone_id=[ZONE_ID]. This API is non-standard—we'll get fixed server side at some point, but for now can address with a PR here.

The issue here is that the VTIMEZONE subcomponent's DTSTART is a date, not a datetime. In most cases we currently don't support invalid VCALENDARs.

Some error reporting would be nice in any case, 

Quote from RFC 5545:

> 
      The mandatory "DTSTART" property gives the effective onset date
      and local time for the time zone sub-component definition.
      "DTSTART" in this usage MUST be specified as a date with a local
      time value.

On 09/02/16 13:55, Matt Williams wrote:
> I guess it come down to what the purpose of |GangaList| /is/. Why do we
> have |GangaList| instead of a normal Python |list|? I can think of a few
> possible things:
>
>  1. So that we can provide a proxy list interface whereby real
>     |GangaObject|s are stored but if you're in GPI-land when requesting
>     items (|mylist[3]|) you get proxied objects.
>  2. Persistency. It makes it easier to put a |GangaList| in a
>     |ComponentItem| and have it know how to store itself. Of course, we
>     could just as easily have the serialisation code say "it's a |list|,
>     serialise as a sequence" instead of "you're a |GangaList|, serialise
>     yourself as a sequence".
>  3. Parents. I'm not sure where we're /supposed/ to be here but I
>     suppose it's good that the |GangaList| can automatically set the
>     parent of all its elements to the |GangaList|'s parent. Of course,
>     we could just as easily do that in the getter and setter with a
>     normal |list|.
>
> All of which come together to mean that I feel that really |GangaList|
> should exist simply as a way of proxy-wrapping a |list| so that
> |addProxy([1,2,3])| give a |GangaList|. And even then it's only needed
> if the list is containing |GangaObject|s.

After careful consideration, we have decided not to incorporate these changes.

We carefully created this library as a reference implementation for a Memento client.  Our intention was to make this library handle all edge cases, especially considering our initial customer was Wikipedia.  For example, if the submitted URI is a URI-M, then if we merely append the URI-M to a TimeGate base URI, the chance is that a 404 will result.  We want the library to provide a high quality URI-M, hence we follow the protocol strictly.

Overriding *functional scoped* `event_loop` fixture with custom one in `conftest.py` with `scope='module'` can help but I really very discourage this solution.

The problem is: if a loop is created *per test* all your tests are **isolated**. After a test run loop can have not finished tasks, open sockets and other scheduled events. These events may raise an exception inside the next test. Debugging the error becomes a nightmare very easy.

Much better way is instead `setup_module()` to use a fixture for motor client like:
```
@pytest.fixture
def motor(event_loop):
    ret = AsyncIOMotorClient(URLl, io_loop=event_loop)
    yield ret
    ret.close()
```

And after this just add `motor` parameter to every test which need the client.

We assume a scenario, that the stock return can be explained by ev(enterprise value) and ni(net income).
So for every stock, the return of stock i 
```
R_i = alpha + beta1 * ev_i + beta2 * ni_i
```
Using all the R_i ev_i and ni_i of all stocks we get a alpha , beta1 and beta2  in the regression model.
If we can get a regression model like this, we can get the conclusion that Return is determined by ev and ni.
So in the stock market we can get the alpha of stock_i between R_i(calculated from ev and ni in the regression equation) and R_i(actual) .

I am the original author of Schematics, so I can say clearly why I made
those decisions.

By not putting those methods inside classes, we don't need to have
instances of classes. One could use a static method, but I didn't want to
encourage any more OO than necessary for the metaprogramming to work.

Recursion simply isn't necessary for exporting fields. It doesn't actually
make sense to me how this would work since fields are stored in a list that
is easily iterated across.

I didn't write the code where we see `if hasattr(field, 'export_loop')`, so
I can't explain that, but I would prefer a cleaner approach here too. I
think maybe the ModelType fields could handle this themselves via calls to
`to_primitive` or `to_native`.

The reason there is no blank `export` or `import` is because I want users
to be aware of whether or not they're converting to python types or to
types that are safe to put in json conversion tools.

To be clear, the transforms module exists because the initial
implementation of the transforms were not attached to the models at all. We
put those functions  in there (well, Jokull and co did) to get users the
functionality with fewer imports.

I found out why `title.replace("$","$$")` is necessary. @beardypig 

It turns out, it's not. In the two media players I tested, (vlc and mpv) they both happen to use $ as a character to denote the start of some replacement in the title.

See https://mpv.io/manual/stable/#property-expansion for mpv and https://wiki.videolan.org/Documentation:Format_String/ for vlc. The fact that both of them use "$$" to mean "$" is a coincidence.

For us to handle this, we would have to write exceptions for each player that streamlink is meant to support. Since streamlink is meant to support an indefinite amount of players, it's not possible to handle this.

So there's two options that come to mind if StreamLink is to support this feature:

1. Ignore Property Expansion / Format String problems and just let whatever player specific format codes that happen to be in streamer's titles get passed to the player unhandled.

Pros: 
- Streamlink can claim to be player agnostic still
- Don't have to support an indefinite amount of players
- Users who expect Property Expansions / Format Strings to work will still have it work (we aren't escaping their intentional codes)

Cons:
- The title as displayed by various streaming sites will not precisely match the window title as intended in rare circumstances where the stream title happens to contain special player specific format codes

2. Escape the various player codes in {title} for the most popular media players

Pros:
- {title} will be formatted in such a way that it is passed to the media player to be displayed exactly how it appears on the actual streaming site

Cons:
- Streamlink will only support this feature "properly" for a limited number of media players
- Tests should probably be written for this, which may be difficult since we can't get the real window title properly without actually running the program and doing something like "xprop -title [expected title]" in linux and something with win32gui in windows. I'd need help writing these tests, since I'm not familiar enough with python-mock to do so, and don't want to learn it for this feature.
- If not written properly, it could interfere with what the user wants to do. e.g. if we simply do something like `if player is 'mpv': title = "?>" + title` to disable all Property Expansion, the user will get mad when they actually try to use Property Expansion

If I can add a few cents to the discussion.
I think that saving only manual changes is the way to move foward. I think one of the expected ways that `set` is supposed to work is that you can set new values and that the software remembers it. I wouldn't want that to change or be ambiguous in any way. So if a user sets a value, it should persist.

Personally I would be more fond of a solution where instead of calling `set` from the commandline, we use normal `--something` looking arguments. For example `--set key=value`. And maintain a separate list of configurations set in this manor. That way we could actually alter the list of configurations outputted to the screen in a way where it reflects which configurations are overwritten by the new arguments. I think it is important that we visually convey that there are temporate settings applied, to the user.

I also think that when a user sets a configuration that is currently being overwritten by a temporary one, we remove the temporary one making the users last value the one that counts.

I don't think there's anything specific to do here.

Instead of supporing arbitrary async context managers, I think we should support arbitrary objects that have `await obj.acquire()` and `obj.release()`. The advantages are (1) the context manager exit protocol is quite complicated when it comes to exception handling and we don't need any of that here, (2) this makes the release a synchronous operation, which turns out to be very useful because the return-to-trio code runs in synchronous context and it seems silly to spawn a whole `Task` just to call some synchronous `__aexit__` methods.

I agree that using stdlib re would be better. 

But if, as you said, "the giant regex will be cut into 1/3 of the original size", stdlib re probably won't be fast enough. I've observed up to 1000x speed difference for this huge regex between stdlib re and re2. If we assume re works in O(N) regarding length of the regex then reducing regex size into 1/3 of the original size will make matching of this regex 3x faster - it is not enough. We could end up with a more complex implementation which is still 300x slower than the existing re2-based.

This is only a speculation, and maybe I'm wrong.

Checking for faults would be nice, but that would be very heuristic (guessing at files that should be there, ...) or heavy (checking for all-NaN files). Checksums obviously do not help here, because the they are generated from the released products on server side as well as on client side and are therefore identical even for faulty releases.

It would be quite cool if `sentinelsat` could remove duplicates already in the OpenAccessHub results based on identical **granule(s)** and **sensing time** (i.e. ignoring the production time). ```
def launchConsumer(i):
    consumer = Consumer({'bootstrap.servers': KAFKA_BROKER, \
    'group.id': 'testConsumer', \
    'enable.auto.commit': True,\
    'default.topic.config': {'auto.offset.reset': 'latest'}})
    topic = TopicPartition(FROM_TOPIC,i)
    print "Topic offset",topic.offset
    consumer.assign([topic])
    print "Getting Position"
    position = consumer.position([topic])
    if position[0].offset<0:
        position[0].offset=0 #Reset Offset
    print "Partition", i, ", Position: ", position
    hasData = True
    try:
        while hasData:
            print "Running..."
            message = consumer.poll()
            if message:
                logger.debug("Consumer: Data Retrieved")
                print "================================================"
                print dumps(loads(message.value()), indent=4)
				msg = loads(message.value())
	            if msg['type']=='insert':
	            	# Do Something
                print "================================================"
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        sys.stderr.write('%% Aborted by user\n')
        logger.debug('%% Aborted by user\n')
        c.close()
    c.close()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    threads = list()
    topic = FROM_TOPIC
	threadCount = int(sys.argv[1])
    for i in range(threadCount):
        t = threading.Thread(target=launchConsumer, args=(i,)) # pass in the callable
        threads.append(t)
        print('Starting Thread {}'.format(i))
        t.start()
    for i, t in enumerate(threads):
        t.join()
        print('Thread {} Stopped'.format(i))
```

I had a look at skimage._shared._geometry. So this is only polygon clipping. I tried to check how many skimage code parts depend on ._shared._geometry. Only `skimage.draw` depends on it directly (the draw.polygon_perimeter function of it uses polygon_clip). The following depend on ._shared._geometry indirectly via skimage.draw:
- skimage/morphology/selem.py (draw.ellipse)
- skimage/transform/_hough_transform.pyx (draw.circle_perimeter)
- many doc examples

The funny thing is, the selem.py and _hough_transform.pyx don't actually use the polygon_clip function, so they are independent of matplotlib if the code would be refactored a bit. In the end it is really just the skimage.draw.polygon_perimeter function which is not used in skimage itself, but of course, who knows which external packages use it.

Regarding `skimage.io`, what about pillow? This is also a dependency and used as an io plugin, right?

About tifffile, the imshow function of it is [not exposed](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/io/_plugins/tifffile_plugin.ini) by the plugin, and inside the tifffile.py file matplotlib is imported lazily, so that shouldn't be a problem I think.

I think none of the things above are real blockers. Now, the more important part, user experience. This is indeed a tricky one. However, I have a feeling that people who use scikit-image use matplotlib anyway, so I would guess that making it an optional dependency would not cause much disruption in the end.

I would be interested what others think as well.

Hi @hmaarrfk 

Thank you for the PR! Normally this would count as a fantastic PR, given the full test coverage with zero prompting from us. However, unfortunately you have duplicated existing functionality:

http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/user_guide/data_types.html

It looks like we need to work on the discoverability of that page, because many, many issues that people raise come from not finding it. If you have any ideas for improving it, we would be very receptive!

The table was out of date in 0.11 from what I can tell, so presumably someone made the call that removing it was less bothersome than maintaining it. I also think that there was a desire to follow our own roadmap instead of "chasing" Matlab. However, I actually think having something like that would be extremely useful, but quite a lot of work to create in the first place, and then to maintain. Still, if you're feeling brave, it would be good to compile.

Currently, much of the functionality is spread across NumPy, SciPy.ndimage, scikit-image, and Matplotlib. For other stuff (such as reading DICOM), you would need other libraries such as NiBabel. However, they certainly exist! So I would probably make a 4-column table, "Matlab", "Core SciPy (includes NumPy, SciPy, Matplotlib", "scikit-image", "third party suggestions". I would also store it as a csv and somehow parse that into HTML, as CSV would be way easier to maintain.

Anyway, sorry for the duplicated work here. I hope that there's other bits of the table that you would be interested in contributing! I made the Matlab -> Python jump myself once upon a time and it's totally worth it. ;) When in doubt, it's a good idea to ping us at scikit-image@python.org or gitter.im/scikit-image/scikit-image, where we can point you in the right direction or confirm that something isn't yet implemented.
