@stefanv, @ahojnnes
Here are a couple of concrete examples.  

In the first example below, when order=0, 1, or 3 `rescale` will eventually call the `_warp_fast` function from `skimage.transform._warps_cy.pyx` which in turn calls functions from `_shared/interpolation.pyx`
The order 2, 4, 5 cases, call scipy.ndimage.map_coordinates() instead.

```python
import numpy as np
from skimage.transform import rescale
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
f, ax = plt.subplots(1, 7)
img = np.ones((7, 7))
img[2:5, 2:5] = 0.0
scale_factor = 2.0
ax[0].imshow(img)
# ax[0].set_axis_off()
ax[0].set_title('original')
for order in range(7):
    ax[order+1].imshow(rescale(img, scale_factor, order=order, mode='constant',
                               preserve_range=False),
                       vmin=0, vmax=1)
    # ax[order+1].set_axis_off()
    ax[order+1].set_title('order={}'.format(order))
```
![interp_example2](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6528957/7710633/31047ec0-fe33-11e4-9fd9-864e33deb0ee.png)
Here the first and last coordinates end up outside the original range and are set to cval for the orders that call scipy, but are interpolated using a pixel value of cval in the skimage cases.

Also, strangely, if the image is all ones, the interpolation is no longer apparent at the edges for order = 1 or 3.  See below:
```python
f, ax = plt.subplots(1, 6)
img = np.ones((4, 4))
img[3:5, 3:5] = 0.0
scale_factor = 2.0
for order in range(6):
    ax[order].imshow(rescale(img, scale_factor, order=order, mode='constant'),
                     vmin=0, vmax=1)
    ax[order].set_axis_off()
    ax[order].set_title('order={}'.format(order))
```

![interp_example](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6528957/7709526/aba392ea-fe2b-11e4-9e3a-7dc8ac0791dd.png)

In this case, the coordinates that are getting passed to `map_coordinates` along each axis are:
[-0.25,  0.25,  0.75,  1.25,  1.75,  2.25,  2.75,  3.25]
 -0.25 and 3.25 are outside the original range of [0, 1, 2, 3], and get mapped to cval=0

There is some default linear and angular damping in btMultiBody. You can disable it using pybullet

pybullet.changeDynamics(bodyUniqueId=..., linkIndex=..., linearDamping=0, angularDamping=0)
Do this for the base (linkIndex=-1) and all links.
See also the [PyBullet quickstart guide](https://docs.google.com/document/d/10sXEhzFRSnvFcl3XxNGhnD4N2SedqwdAvK3dsihxVUA/edit#).

Why do you want to use Roboschool instead of PyBullet?
PyBullet is more actively developed, much easier to install, and it exposes the full PhysicsClient_CAPI.

One tricky aspect for us was switching the joint damping constraint on and off at joint limits (see [ODEJoint::ApplyImplicitStiffnessDamping](https://bitbucket.org/osrf/gazebo/src/783d80d6567cebb1d350296145a87f311acfb38f/gazebo/physics/ode/ODEJoint.cc#cl-842)).

Hi @svensht2 

There is the subject of per-frame metadata that is required for proper synchronization. Without hardware timestamps what you get are software estimates and there is nothing preventing the streams from drifting.  Unfortunately both Windows and Linux does not offer this feature out of the box yet. You can read more about it [here](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/doc/frame_metadata.md#os-support), including how to enable it. 

In addition, there is a trade-off between latency and frame-drops, especially when the system is under heavy stress. In such conditions, we can either increase queue sizes (introducing larger potential latency) or suffer significant frame drops making it almost impossible to pick up well-synchronized pairs (freezes). 
In the align example, we relax the synchronization requirement and let the sample use not perfectly matched pairs in absence of better options. We are planning to make that configurable. 

In practice, changing `false` to `true` in [image.cpp:785](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/src/image.cpp#L785) is likely to reduce latency. Compiling as Release can also improve the situation. We are going to publish a more general patch very soon to address many of these issues. 

As for hardware synchronization, if I recall correctly hardware sync between depth and color is automatically enabled when sensors are configured to the same FPS.

>  2. Add a new message type for direct communication between nodes, in this case it would be negotiation of an identifier for the transfer.

Are you talking about a new message that could be used without the payment protocol? That is possible but I think it complicate things, there are quite a few problems:

- should this new message be sent prior or after the payment?
  - if prior, for how long should the node store this metadata in memory? (We don't have indexed tables to query for this metadata from the database)
  - if after, for how long should the node wait for the metadata?
- to avoid attacks this message must be authenticated, which introduces an additional signature

Instead of adding a new generic communication message, I'm proprosing replacing the message at the step 3 below (but not messages from step 4 and 5) with a new message that has this metadata:

1. Alices sends a MediatedTransfer to Bob with Charlie as target
2. Bob mediates the transfer, and sends a MediatedTransfer to Charlie
2. Charlie sends a SecretRequest to Alice
3. Alice sends a *SecretReveal* to Charlie
4. Charlie sends a *SecretReveal* to Bob
5. Bob sends a *SecretReveal* to Alice
6/7 Alice sends an Secret message to Bob and Bob to Charlie

Yes, from my perspective, exposing/requiring this idempotent interface to the user adds an unecessary burden on users, and increase complexity from user's perspective/interface building. The identifier is already there, we wouldn't even need to change user's exposed APIs.
I would add that, besides ignoring requests with already in flight message sending, we should keep some registry so, for timed-out (or other somehow failed message sending, e.g.: no ACK received) and same ID request, we should go for the same final balance signing of previous request (understanding that `same id == same balance`), instead of re-incrementing and signing a new higher balance, and, oc, sending again this message. Users requesting a new transfer with different balance and same `identifier` is an error.

Hi @pengyu,

Removing consecutive whitespaces (except newlines) do not have an effect on training, as consecutive whitespaces are treated as one whitespace by fastText (i.e. additional whitespaces are ignored).

FastText uses newlines to separate examples, and words are not sampled across lines (please see #518 for a longer answer regarding newlines). In practice, removing or keeping newlines does not have a big impact usually. We thus recommend to keep newlines when training word vectors.

Best,
Edouard.

Here are my thoughts for foreign key use. 

From a data publisher perspective:

1. Too many codes

  - I have a list of codes in my table
  - there are too many to put in an enum Constraint for   
  - I create a separate look up table and use FK for validation

2. What does the code mean?

  - I have a list of codes in my table
  - without documentation they are meaningless 
  - I create a separate look up table with the code and description 
  - use FK for validation

3. Consistent codes for multiple datasets (novice)

  - I have a set of codes I want everyone to use
  - They're important so I got them published as an open .CSV (I'm a novice and don't know about data packages yet)
  - I ask my peer organisations to use the codes in their data publications 
  - Some of my peers know about data packages, others don't 
  - I need to easily discover instructions, examples, and tools to help me and others publish and use the shared code list
  - for peer organisations that understand data packages, could they reference the external CSV and create a FK relationship?

4. Consistent codes for multiple datasets

  - I have a set of codes I want everyone to use
  - They're important so I've got them published online as data package
  - I ask my peer organisations to use the codes in their data publications 
  - They use external FK relationship in their data packages to comply

5. Consistent codes but breaking change

  - I have a set of codes I want everyone to use
  - They're important so I've got them published as a data package
  - My peer organisation are using the codes in their data publications 
  - I have to make major changes to the code list. Some codes are no longer valid.
  - How do I tell others what's happening (who is using my code list?)
  - How do I safely make the change (versions, date ranges on codes?)
  - What do I tell users of code-list to do? 

Thinking as a Data Package Tool Provider:

6. Help users understand the meaning of codes

  - The table displayed has a column with codes
  - The codes and their meaning have a FK relationship to this column
  - When I hover my mouse over the code, a tooltip showing its meaning is displayed
  - When a user clicks the code, show the related row in the FK table (where they may discover more than just the meaning of the code)

As a data consumer:

7. Make it easily for me to download 

  - I'm comforted by the effort you've taken to validate the data and apply consistent codes
  - your efforts give me confidence to explore the data further
  - give it to me in one big dereferenced table so I can explore it using tools I know like Excel.

You cannot create a notification to publish to a given Cloud Pub/Sub topic until the following conditions are met:
 1.) the topic exists.
 2.) the GCS service account associated with the project that owns this bucket has permission to publish to that topic.

GCS enforces this constraint, which is what you're seeing. The error message is correct.

@ppawiggers Note the different error codes:  yours is `UNAVAILABLE`, which indicates a transient back-end failure (should be auto-retried).  The error reported by @ensonic is `UNAUTHENTICATED`, which indicates that the credentials associated with the client expired and were not auto-refreshed.

By default, you must assume nothing in this library (or any other one, for that matter), to be thread-safe.
If done anyway, each thread should at least have its own repository instance. Then the contention will be moved to the file-system, which at least _should_ be OK due to the usage of lock-files.
However, I do advice against trying to write and read at the same time from multiple threads, as it is asking for trouble.

@nvie Thanks for bringing this up ! I believe it's one of the worst decisions made in GitPython to attempt to decode everything using UTF-8 (or sometimes using whatever encoding Git hints us at). Especially for paths, doing this is plain wrong as the filesystem may use arbitrary encodings. This is the reason Rust uses [OsStrings](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/ffi/struct.OsString.html) to represent these, as opposed to [Strings](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/string/struct.String.html) which need to be valid UTF-8.
Unfortunately, back in the days Rust wasn't on my Radar, and also I didn't know what I know now.

That said, fixing the API in that regard might be a somewhat major undertaking certainly worth making. Without a major version upgrade, the proposed adjustment might be good enough.

It should test all code cells - that includes cells containing cell magics like `%%timeit`. It doesn't do anything with markdown cells.

Both `--nbval` and `--nbval-lax` will run all code cells, and check that they run without an error. With `--nbval`, it checks the output of all cells that don't have an `# NBVAL_IGNORE_OUTPUT` comment. With `--nbval-lax`, it only checks the output for cells that do have `# NBVAL_CHECK_OUTPUT`.
